---
title: "4.1.3 Running of applications"
subtitle: "Software P160-P161"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        .reveal .figure-placeholder { border: 1px dashed #8aa0bf; border-radius: 8px; background: #f4f8ff; color: #40526b; padding: 1.1em 1.3em; margin: 0.8em 0; font-size: 0.9em; text-align: center; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.74em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.34em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 4 Directory / 返回 Unit 4 目录](../../index.html)

## Running of Applications

### Operating system as a platform

Application software requires the <span class="term">operating system</span> (操作系统) to provide a <span class="mark">platform</span> on which the software can run successfully.

This section brings together earlier ideas about:

::: {.tight-list}
- hardware
- firmware
- the operating system
- application software
:::

## Booting Up

### Loading the operating system into RAM

When a computer starts up, part of the operating system needs to be loaded into <span class="term">RAM</span>.

This is known as:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">booting up</span> (启动)
- or using a <span class="term">bootstrap loader</span> (引导加载程序)
:::

## BIOS

### Finding and loading the operating system

The start-up of the computer's motherboard is handled by the <span class="term">basic input/output system (BIOS)</span>.

The BIOS:

::: {.tight-list}
- tells the computer where the storage device holding the operating system can be found
- loads the part of the operating system that is needed
- executes that part of the operating system
:::

## Firmware

### BIOS as firmware

The BIOS is often referred to as <span class="term">firmware</span> (固件).

<span class="term">Firmware</span> is defined as a program that provides <span class="mark">low level control</span> for devices.

::: {.figure-placeholder}
Figure 4.11 Firmware interface between OS and hardware screenshot placeholder
:::

## BIOS Storage

### EEPROM and CMOS

The BIOS program is stored in a special type of ROM called <span class="term">EEPROM</span> (Electrically Erasable Programmable ROM).

The BIOS settings are stored on a <span class="term">CMOS</span> chip (Complementary Metal Oxide Semi-conductor).

::: {.tight-list}
- EEPROM contents remain when the computer is powered down
- BIOS can be rewritten, updated or deleted by a user
- CMOS is powered by a rechargeable battery on the motherboard
:::

## BIOS Settings

### What happens if CMOS power is lost

If the CMOS battery is removed or disconnected, the BIOS settings would be reset.

Once the CMOS is restarted:

::: {.tight-list}
- it accesses the same BIOS program from EEPROM
- the settings become the <span class="mark">default factory settings</span>
- user changes such as clock speed settings are lost
:::

## Applications While Running

### OS control and system software

Application software is under the control of the operating system.

While it is running, application software may need to:

::: {.tight-list}
- access system software such as <span class="term">device drivers</span>
- use parts of the operating system loaded into RAM
- have different parts of the operating system loaded in and out of RAM
:::

## Classroom Check

### Explain how applications start and run

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- application software requires the operating system as a <span class="mark">platform</span>
- part of the operating system is loaded into <span class="term">RAM</span> during booting
- <span class="term">BIOS</span> finds and loads the required operating system part
- firmware provides <span class="mark">low level control</span> for devices
- BIOS program is stored in EEPROM; BIOS settings are stored in CMOS
- applications may access system software, such as device drivers, while running
:::

## Bridge

### Next: Interrupts

The next deck explains interrupt signals, buffers and interrupt service routines.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 4 Directory / 返回 Unit 4 目录](../../index.html)
